1D · Disputes
🔄

Revision

Lesson 1 · Warm-up — Exaggeration recap

⏱ 7 min

Use exaggeration to talk about your week! 💬

Tell your teacher about something that happened this week — use at least 2 techniques from last lesson.

📈 Inflate amounts🔁 and…and…and 🖼 like / as if💀 Death metaphors
Conversation starters — pick one
⏳ TimeSomething that took forever, or that you had to do again and again
😩 EnergySomething exhausting you couldn't wait to finish
😂 FunnySomething amusing — make it dramatic!
💸 Cost / EffortSomething that took a lot of effort, money or time
Quick-fire check ✅ — your teacher listens for all four
📈 Inflating amounts"I spent half my life…" / "It cost a small fortune…"
🔁 Repetition with and"She talked and talked and talked…"
🖼 Comparisons"It was like the North Pole in there!"
💀 Death metaphors"I'm dying for a coffee." / "It's murder."
🔄

Warm-up & Lead-in

Lesson 1 · Vocabulary: Fights & Disputes

⏱ 7 min

Quick revision — exaggeration check 💬

Can you still use the four techniques from last lesson? Talk about your week with your teacher — use at least 2 techniques!

📈 Inflate amounts🔁 and…and…and 🖼 like / as if💀 Death metaphors
Lead-in — discuss
1Think of the last time you had an argument or disagreement with someone. What was it about?
2What kinds of situations make you most likely to lose your temper?
3Are you more of a person who confronts conflict directly or avoids it?
Today's goal

By the end of this lesson you'll be able to describe different types of disputes, use 8 key phrases connected to arguments, and talk about conflict in natural, idiomatic English.

💬

Types of Disputes

Vocabulary presentation

⏱ 8 min
Look at these photos (A–D) — what disputes could happen here?
✈ A — Airport / on a flight
✈
🍽 B — Restaurant
🏠 C — Apartment block
🚗 D — Car rental / hotel check-in

Discuss: what argument might be happening in each photo?

Six types of dispute — learn these
a workplace disputeargument between colleagues or with management, often about conditions or behaviour
a domestic disputeargument between people who live together (partners, flatmates, family)
a pay disputedisagreement about wages, salary, or working conditions — can lead to strikes
a dispute between neighboursargument between people who live close to each other
a row between friendsan angry argument between friends — row = /raʊ/ (British English)
mediating in a disputeacting as a neutral third party to help two sides reach an agreement
Quick check — can you give an example of each from real life?

Think of something you've seen, heard about, or experienced for at least 3 of these types.

🔑

Key Phrases

Vocabulary: verbs & expressions

⏱ 5 min
Study these 8 phrases — they all appear in today's exercises
clear the airresolve a misunderstanding or tension through open conversation
on speaking termstalking to each other normally (often used in negative: not on speaking terms)
side with sbsupport one person in a dispute rather than staying neutral
escalate(of a situation) get worse or more serious
come to blowsstart fighting physically; or reach a point of extreme conflict
kick sb outforce someone to leave a place or relationship
quarrelargue repeatedly, especially about small things
willing to compromiseprepared to give up something in order to reach an agreement
Ongoing dispute vs. ongoing argument — what's the difference?

Ongoing dispute suggests a longer, more formal or serious disagreement. Ongoing argument is more casual/personal. Both mean the conflict hasn't been resolved.

🔗

Match the Situations

Exercise A

⏱ 7 min
📚 Vocab
Match each comment (1–6) to a type of dispute. Click a comment, then click the matching type.
Comments
1 The tension had been building up in the office so we had a meeting to try and clear the air.
2 They were hardly on speaking terms for a while … but thankfully they've made up now!
3 We're not here to side with anyone. We're here to resolve this before it escalates.
4 They've had an ongoing dispute about a parking space and nearly came to blows.
5 They've always quarrelled a lot, but it seems she's actually kicked him out this time.
6 Hopefully it won't end up in a strike, but they've not been willing to compromise.
Types
a dispute between neighbours
a workplace dispute
a domestic dispute
a pay dispute
mediating in a dispute
a row between friends

Flatmate Drama

Exercise C — contextual gap fill

⏱ 8 min
📚 Vocab in context
Fill the gaps using the correct form of the words below. Each word is used once.
escalated speaking terms clear the air side with tension mediate compromise

Last semester, there was a lot of in our student flat. My flatmate and I hadn't been on for weeks after an argument about the dishes. Eventually, our friend Emma stepped in to in the dispute. She was completely neutral — she refused to either of us. She suggested we try to reach a by setting a cleaning schedule. The situation nearly when my flatmate threatened to move out, but in the end we managed to and actually became much better friends after that.

Discuss
→Have you ever been in a similar situation with a flatmate, friend or family member? What happened?
→Was there ever a time when someone mediated in one of your disputes? Did it help?
✏️

Replace & Reflect

Exercise D — adapted from the book

⏱ 7 min
📚 Vocab
Replace the words in italics with the correct form of the target vocabulary
1Are you someone who is prepared to give up some of what you want?
→ someone who is
2Have you heard of a dispute that quickly got worse and where people then physically fought?
→ that and where people ?
3Do you ever support one person more than the other?
→ do you ever one person?
4Do you know anyone who was forced to leave?
→ who was ?
5Do you know people who often argue about small things repeatedly?
→ who often ?
6Have you heard of a conflict between two sides that has lasted a long time?
→ an ?
Now answer 3 of those questions honestly — share with your teacher!

Use the vocabulary in your answers. E.g. "Yes, I know people who quarrel constantly — my cousins nearly came to blows last Christmas..."

🗣

Speaking: Dispute Stories

Exercise E — fluency & vocab consolidation

⏱ 10 min
🗣 Speaking

Tell me about a dispute! 🎤

Tell your teacher about a real dispute you know about (or invent one). It can be funny, dramatic, or completely ordinary. Aim for 2–3 minutes.

Tick off each word from today as you use it ↓

ongoing dispute
escalate
side with
come to blows
clear the air
on speaking terms
willing to compromise
kick sb out
quarrel
mediate
Conversation starters — pick one or combine
🏠 Flatmates / familyA dispute at home — who was involved, what was it about, how did it end?
👯 Friend group dramaA row that split a friend group — who sided with whom?
🛍 Customer complaintA time you (or someone you know) had a dispute in a shop, restaurant, or online
📱 Social mediaA public argument or cancellation you followed online — did it escalate?
Useful language to tell your story
→"It all started when …"
→"The tension had been building for a while because …"
→"Things really escalated when …"
→"In the end, they / we managed to … / never quite …"
🎭

What Would You Do?

Lesson 2 · Lead-in

⏱ 5 min
Vocab check — quick recap of Lesson 1

Without looking at your notes — what do these mean?

clear the air
come to blows
willing to compromise
mediate in a dispute

What would you do? 🤔

Discuss these situations — what would be the best way to handle each one?

🚗 Car rentalYou arrive to pick up your car but there's "no record" of your booking — you paid online
🏨 HotelYou booked a double room but the receptionist gives you two singles "because that's all that's left"
✈ AirportYour checked bag is 0.5kg over the limit and they want to charge you €50
📦 Online orderThe item arrived damaged but the company refuses to refund you, saying you caused the damage
Today's focus

In this lesson we'll look at how to handle disputes in English — listening to real conversations, learning useful phrases for different roles (customer, employee, mediator), and practising sounding firm when you need to.

🎧

Listening 1

Exercise 5 · First listen

⏱ 10 min
🎧 Listening
🔊 Track 1.9 — Three people trying to resolve a dispute. Listen once through first, then answer the questions.
Before you listen — predict

Based on the lead-in, which scenario do you think it is? What do you predict the problem will be?

Exercise 5 — Answer after listening
1Where are they?
2What's the problem?
3What has caused the problem?
4How is the dispute resolved in the end?
Comprehension check — discuss
→Which person do you think handled the situation best? Why?
→Was the resolution fair? What would you have done differently?
📋

Listen Again

Exercise 6 — Complete the sentences

⏱ 8 min
🎧 Listening + Vocab
🔊 Track 1.10 — Listen again and complete each sentence with 2–3 words.
Exercise 6 — Fill the gaps (2–3 words)
1With all , that's your problem. Not ours.
2I'm awfully sorry about all of this and obviously if to me …
3So can we talk to whoever's here?
4I understand that this may not be down to you, but this is .
5Could you please not at me? I'm just doing my job.
6If you could just try and a bit …
7I mean, there must be some way we can here, surely.
Notice — who said what?

Look at sentences 1–7. Which ones were said by the customer, and which by the employee? What's different about the language each uses?

🗂

Useful Phrases

Exercise 7 — Sort into categories

⏱ 7 min
📚 Phrases
Add sentences from Exercise 6 to the correct category. Click a phrase to place it.
With all due respect, that's your problem. I'm awfully sorry about all of this … Can we talk to whoever's in charge? This is unacceptable. Could you please not shout at me? If you could just try and calm down a bit … There must be some way we can sort this out.

🤝 Seeking compromise

Is there really nothing you can do? / Would it be possible to …?

😤 Expressing annoyance

There must be some mistake. / You've got to be kidding me!

🙏 Apologising

I'm afraid the manager is not around at the moment.

😠 Dealing with anger

You're really not making this any easier. / I do understand …
💡

Phrases in Use

Exercise 7b — NEW: choose the right phrase

⏱ 6 min
📚 Phrases
Choose the most appropriate phrase for each situation
1 🛒 A customer is getting increasingly angry and raising their voice at you.
2 ✈ You want to speak to someone with more authority — the person you're talking to can't help.
3 🏨 You made a mistake with a customer's reservation. They're understandably upset.
4 🚗 You've explained your situation and want to find a way forward that works for both sides.
💪

Sounding Firm

Grammar focus — stressed auxiliaries

⏱ 8 min
✏ Grammar

📌 How to sound more forceful in disputes

When we disagree with someone or want to insist on something, we stress the auxiliary verb. This makes the statement sound more firm and assertive.

When there is no auxiliary verb, we add do / does / did to carry the stress.

I asked you not to enter. → I did ask you not to enter.
I understand. → I do understand.
🔊 Track 1.10 — Listen to these sentences. Where is the stress?
Exercise 8 — Notice the stressed auxiliary
1I do understand that you might be upset.
2I am only doing my job.
Make these sentences more forceful — add do/did or stress the auxiliary
1I told you to call ahead. → I you to call ahead.
2I want to help you. → I to help you.
3I booked this room six weeks ago. → I this room six weeks ago.
🎤

Say It Like You Mean It

Exercise 9 — Stress practice

⏱ 6 min
🗣 Speaking
Say these sentences as forcefully as possible — stress the underlined word. Work in pairs.
1I'm afraid I am going to need to see some ID.
2I did talk to someone about this last week on the phone.
3I'm afraid I am going to have to ask you to leave.
4You are going to need to come back tomorrow.
5I do want to sort this out, but you're not making it easy.
6I have warned you about this twice now.
7You really do have to return the keys today.
8We are going to pay, but only once everything's sorted.
Now try without the underline — listen to your partner and spot which word they stress

Take turns: one reads, one listens and says which auxiliary they heard stressed. Does the stress change the feel of the sentence?

🎧

Listening 2

Exercise 10 & 11

⏱ 8 min
🎧 Listening
🔊 Track 1.12 — Another dispute. Listen and decide if each statement is True or False.
Exercise 10 — True (T) or False (F)?
1The couple are buying a second-hand car.
2They're planning to split the driving on their journey equally.
3The man they're talking to needs to see some documents.
4There's no record of their booking.
5Adding an extra driver will cost them 100 euros a day.
6They're not staying nearby.
7They pay cash to cover the extra charge.
8They manage to reach a compromise.
🔊 Track 1.12 again — now for Exercise 11.
Exercise 11 — Find the difference

These sentences are slightly different from what the speakers said. Listen and identify what was actually said (look for stressed auxiliaries!).

1There must be a mistake. I'm absolutely positive I put both names down.
2You've got to be kidding!
3I really am awfully sorry about this.
4I understand you might be upset, but could you please not raise your voice, sir?
5I am going to have to ask you for an extra 100 euros.
🎭

Roleplay

Exercises 12 & 13 — Discussion & Speaking

⏱ 10 min
🗣 Speaking
Exercise 12 — Discuss first
1How did the people in Listening 1 and 2 handle their situations? Would you have done anything differently?
2Were the compromises they reached fair?
3Have you ever had a dispute while travelling or shopping? What happened?
Exercise 13 — Roleplay: negotiate a solution

Take turns playing each role. Use the useful phrases! Aim for a real compromise — don't give in too easily.

AHotel check-in gone wrong

A guest arrives at the hotel she booked for a solo trip. The receptionist tells her that the single room she booked has been given away by mistake and only a double room is available — at twice the price. The hotel is fully booked, it's 11pm, and she's exhausted.

Student A — Guest: firm but open to compromise; use "I did book…" and "there must be some way…" Student B — Receptionist: apologise, deal with frustration, offer something
BOnline order disaster

Someone ordered a dress for a friend's wedding — it arrived three days late (after the event) and in the wrong colour. She's calling customer service demanding a full refund. The agent's policy only allows store credit.

Student A — Customer: express annoyance, seek compromise, use "this is unacceptable" + "I did order…" Student B — Agent: apologise sincerely, deal with anger, find middle ground
CFlatmate conflict

Two uni flatmates haven't been on speaking terms for a week after one of them had people over until 3am during exam season. Their mutual friend has asked them to meet and sort it out.

Student A — Unhappy flatmate: firm but willing to compromise; use "I did ask you…" Student B — Other flatmate: apologise, deal with tension, propose a fair solution
Useful phrases — quick reference

🤝 Seeking compromise

  • Is there really nothing you can do?
  • Would it be possible to …?
  • There must be some way we can sort this out.
  • Can we talk to whoever's in charge?

😤 Expressing annoyance

  • There must be some mistake.
  • You've got to be kidding me!
  • With all due respect, …
  • This is completely unacceptable.

🙏 Apologising

  • I'm awfully sorry about all of this.
  • I really am awfully sorry.
  • Obviously if it were up to me …

😠 Dealing with anger

  • I do understand you might be upset, but …
  • You're really not making this any easier.
  • Could you please not shout at me?
  • If you could just calm down a bit …
